title: An AI Model Fit For Purpose
gist: As AI/LLMs increasingly generate the surrogate and behavioral models used in EDA design and verification, the central risk is using a model outside its intended context. Trust must precede adoption via repeatable, traceable processes, metadata tagging/watermarking, holdout validation, and mandatory human sign-off - and today's benchmarks (e.g., VerilogEval) fail to capture industrial complexity, with reliability fundamentally bounded by training-data distribution.

insights.claim: All models are compromises that sacrifice detail for execution speed and only remain valid within the context they were designed for, so using an AI-generated model out of context produces misleading information and potentially expensive escapes into products.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: The central governance principle for AI in EDA - context-scoping determines whether AI models add or destroy value.
insights.quote: Each abstraction works so long as it is not used out of the context for which it was designed.

insights.claim: If AI starts creating models, trust must precede adoption and only emerges from a repeatable and traceable process, with metadata tagging essential to define each model's scope and suitability.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Traceability and tagging become gating requirements for AI-EDA adoption - a checklist for evaluating vendor tools.
insights.quote: If AI starts creating models, trust must precede adoption, and that only happens when a repeatable and traceable process is used.

themes.theme: Existing AI-EDA optimization tools and their limits

insights.claim: Synopsys DSO.ai and Cadence Cerebrus use reinforcement learning to create optimization loops for block-based physical implementation, measuring PPA after each trial to inform better tool-setting choices - and they work because their primary input, the RTL design model, is fairly stable at that flow stage.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Explains why AI works well in implementation (stable inputs) but is riskier in verification (moving targets) - a segmentation of where AI-EDA delivers reliably.
insights.quote: These create optimization loops for block-based physical implementation tools based on reinforcement learning.
insights.locator: Examples of tool and flow modeling

insights.claim: Many AI models today target unstable areas of the flow like verification, where the goal is to find design/testbench errors while converging on a coverage target that may itself be moving, making data correctness and lifetime a critical concern.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Verification's moving-target nature is where AI-model risk concentrates - the harder, higher-value frontier for AI-EDA vendors.
insights.quote: Many of the AI models being created today are tackling areas of the design flow where things are not so stable, such as verification.
insights.locator: The primary input for these implementation

themes.theme: Accuracy vs. completeness - two distinct failure modes

insights.claim: Accuracy and completeness are different, independent model properties: a model can be highly accurate on everything it covers yet deeply incomplete (no behavior defined for important scenarios), and the two failure modes require different remedies.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: A diligence framework - evaluators must test both dimensions, since accuracy metrics alone hide coverage gaps that cause re-spins.
insights.quote: You can have a highly accurate model that is deeply incomplete

insights.claim: AI models in digital verification should be treated as advisory flow models - they can recommend regressions, prioritize tests, or suggest assertions but must not declare closure on their own, staying inside a bounded decision loop where simulation, emulation, or formal results remain the final authority.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Defines the safe deployment envelope for AI verification tools - advisory, not sign-off - which caps liability and shapes product claims.
insights.quote: they should not be allowed to declare closure on their own

insights.claim: The quality of the training distribution matters as much as data volume: critical failure modes (timing violations, protocol corner cases) are systematically underrepresented in typical simulation traces, so a model trained on that data is confidently accurate on common cases and silently wrong on the cases that cause re-spins.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Deliberate corner-case sampling is as important as model fit - a hidden risk in naively-trained AI-EDA tools that investors/buyers should probe.
insights.quote: A model trained on that data will be confidently accurate on common cases, and silently wrong on the cases that cause re-spins.

insights.claim: Forcing models into very compact representations (small bottleneck layers) improves generalization because it pushes the model to discover the real underlying physics rather than memorizing millions of rules of thumb, as demonstrated by a strong Google weather model.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: A technique heuristic - compact/under-parameterized models may be more trustworthy for physics-facing EDA surrogates.
insights.quote: If you can reduce to very compact representations, you are more likely to generalize because you probably learned something more fundamental

themes.theme: Benchmarks are inadequate for industrial complexity

insights.claim: Accuracy for AI-generated design content requires distinguishing three non-equivalent properties - syntactic correctness, functional correctness, and formal completeness - and treating them as interchangeable is one of the most consequential evaluation errors a team can make.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: A rigorous evaluation taxonomy for AI-generated RTL - conflating the three inflates perceived model quality.
insights.quote: Treating these as interchangeable is one of the most consequential evaluation errors a team can make.
insights.locator: None of the generation tools available

insights.claim: Current LLMs achieve near-perfect syntactic correctness on standard Verilog but benchmarks like VerilogEval are dominated by short pedagogical modules; a model scoring 90% on VerilogEval may never have been trained on a cache controller with non-trivial CDC handling, so industrial-scale benchmarks that find where AI breaks do not yet exist.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: High benchmark scores are misleading for real SoC work - a caution against over-trusting vendor benchmark claims.
insights.quote: A model scoring 90% on VerilogEval may never have been trained on a cache controller with non-trivial CDC handling.

insights.claim: LLMs write C or Python that runs accurately out of the box, but SystemVerilog assertions and complex UVM are hit-or-miss partly because the SystemVerilog standard itself is loose and imperfect, so tools give only a best attempt.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: AI code-gen reliability varies sharply by language/domain - verification languages remain a weak spot, tempering automation claims.
insights.quote: if I am talking about SystemVerilog assertions or really complex UVM, it's going to be hit or miss
insights.locator: We can expect varying degrees of correctness

themes.theme: Validation discipline: holdout, physicality, human sign-off

insights.claim: A common fatal mistake is using the same examples for validation that were used for training; proper practice keeps a holdout set, and for physical surrogates validates that the model stays physically consistent (e.g., a passive filter model remains passive and causal - cannot traverse backward in time).
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Concrete validation litmus tests (holdout, passivity, causality) to demand from AI-EDA tooling.
insights.quote: keep a holdout set so that you can validate whether your model matches the holdout set

insights.claim: AI agents can dramatically reduce the iteration burden through automated gap detection (comparing model against golden to find highest-error regions), active learning (selecting the next simulation point that maximizes information gain), and re-creating the model without engineer intervention.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Agentic gap-detection/active-learning is an emerging efficiency frontier for model creation - a productization direction for AI-EDA startups.
insights.quote: agents can intelligently select the next simulation point that maximizes information gain
insights.locator: When inaccuracies, errors, or problems are found

insights.claim: AI models often learn from historical data, so they are better at finding more of what a team has already seen than at exposing genuinely novel bugs, and coverage growth is not the same as correctness - strong-looking metrics can still hide important escapes.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: A caution that AI verification may create false confidence on novel corner cases - the exact failures that matter most.
insights.quote: coverage is not the same as correctness, and strong-looking metrics can still hide important escapes
insights.locator: Alternative flows attempt to incrementally build

insights.claim: Human engineers must sign off on model validity before an agent is permitted to terminate, and every model and its outputs should be traceable and reproducible - keeping mandatory human gates in the AI model-generation loop.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Reinforces human-gated, reproducible AI workflows as the near-term norm - not full autonomy.
insights.quote: Human engineers are then required to sign off on the validity of the model before the agent is permitted to terminate.
insights.locator: There are various ways to help identify holes

themes.theme: Deployment: tagging, watermarking, and bounded trust

insights.claim: Deploying an AI model requires a deliberate boundary-probing exercise on the most complex, novel design patterns to identify where the training distribution ends and the model's uncertainty begins - without it, deployment proceeds without the information needed to manage risk.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: A pre-deployment risk protocol buyers should require - maps the model's safe operating region.
insights.quote: Every team deploying these tools should run a deliberate boundary-probing exercise on a representative sample of their most complex and novel design patterns

insights.claim: AI-generated content should be watermarked and rigorously data-labeled - Siemens EDA notes Google released a standard for flagging AI-generated content, and fine-tuning data must be authenticated and validated before use, requiring discipline from the source onward.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: Provenance/watermarking standards are emerging governance requirements for AI-EDA content - a compliance vector.
insights.quote: It is important to put a watermark on any AI-generated content
insights.locator: It can get complicated when there are restrictions

insights.claim: Even critical AI-generated models should not be trusted 100% and are used in hybrid form - e.g., 50 AI-model evaluations paired with one true ground-truth run - to continuously validate that outputs stay within expected bounds over time.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: A practical hybrid-trust operating pattern (periodic ground-truth checks) for deploying AI models safely.
insights.quote: you do, for instance, 50 evaluations of the AI model, and one with the true ground truth

insights.claim: AI-EDA reliability is bounded by training-data distribution - a well-understood ML constraint rarely documented in vendor datasheets - and most vendor documentation does not specify training scope, failure modes, or domain boundaries, gaps that must close before AI-generated content scales into safety-critical designs.
insights.speaker: Brian Bailey
insights.so_what: A documentation/transparency gap in AI-EDA tools is a barrier to safety-critical adoption - watch which vendors close it first.
insights.quote: reliability is bounded by training data distribution
insights.locator: Without trust, adoption will be limited
